schools.summary: The symbol: 'and the fifth angel sounded, and I saw a star (aster) fall (peptokota — fallen) from heaven unto the earth: and to him was given the key of the bottomless pit (the abyss)' — at the fifth trumpet a star seen fallen to earth, the star (described as 'him') a personal being not a literal star (for to 'him' a key is given); 'to him was given' marking divine permission (the fallen being permitted to unlock the abyss, the key given not seized, even the powers of the pit loosed only by God's allowance). In the application to history, Barnes (with Gill and the historicist tradition) reads the star as a fallen leader or the rise of a false religion, many historicists applying the whole fifth-trumpet vision to Mahomet and the Saracen invasions.

Bede (idealist): the star is the great enemy, Satan, who fell like lightning from heaven (Luke 10:18) and opens the heart of his own by blasphemous, heretical doctrine; the smoke from the pit is the madness of heresy darkening the minds even of the lofty, and the locusts that follow are heretics. Victorinus reads in the recapitulatory frame (the trumpets re-treading the same history), the loosed powers ultimately serving Antichrist.

themes: the fifth angel sounded, and I saw a star fall from heaven unto the earth
to him was given the key of the bottomless pit
the fallen star given the key of the bottomless pit
the abyss loosed only by divine permission

schools.summary: The rising smoke: 'and he opened the bottomless pit (the abyss); and there arose a smoke out of the pit, as the smoke of a great furnace; and the sun and the air were darkened by reason of the smoke of the pit' — when the pit is opened, a vast volume of smoke pouring out like that of a great furnace (thick, abundant, obscuring), the figure of a great darkening cloud arising from the depths; the smoke obscuring the sun and air (the figure of spiritual or political darkness spreading); in the historicist reading, the rise and spread of a darkening error or invading power, the darkness brought by a false religion or invading host (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Bede (idealist): the smoke from the pit is the madness of heresy, which rages to announce beforehand the great furnace of the last persecution and labours to obscure not only the weak but the light of the lofty (though unable to extinguish it); the darkening of sun and air is the clouding of the light of truth by heretical blindness.

themes: he opened the bottomless pit; and there arose a smoke out of the pit, as the smoke of a great furnace
the sun and the air were darkened by reason of the smoke of the pit
the smoke from the abyss darkening the sun and air
the figure of spreading darkness from the depths

schools.summary: The emergence and power: 'and there came out of the smoke locusts (akrides) upon the earth: and unto them was given power, as the scorpions of the earth have power' — from the darkening smoke a swarm of locusts (the proverbial symbol of a vast, devastating, devouring host, Joel 1-2; Exodus 10), given not the ordinary power of locusts (to devour vegetation) but the stinging, tormenting power of scorpions (their hurt the painful sting, not destruction of crops, vv.4-5). Barnes (with Gill and the historicist tradition) reads the locusts as a symbol of an invading host, most historicists applying them to the Saracen/Arab armies of Mahomet swarming out of Arabia (the locust the emblem of the Orient and of an all-devouring army, the scorpion-sting the spread of a tormenting false religion).

Bede (idealist): the locusts are heretics — men (shown by v.4, sent not against grass but against men) who, as the saints are the body of Christ, are the members of the dragon, born one from another from the smoke of heretical blindness; harmless in countenance but keeping poison in their hinder parts, stinging with their mouths and tails. A futurist reading takes the locusts as a literal future demonic plague released from the abyss; Victorinus reads in the recapitulatory frame.

debates.question: What do the locusts from the abyss (and the fifth-trumpet vision) represent?

sides.position: Historicist: the Saracen/Arab armies of Mahomet (a false religion swarming out of the East to torment Christendom)
Idealist/spiritual: heretics, or spiritual torments — the brood of heresy from the smoke of error
Futurist: a literal future plague of demonic beings released from the abyss

themes: there came out of the smoke locusts upon the earth
unto them was given power, as the scorpions of the earth have power
the locusts from the smoke given scorpion power
the crux of the fifth trumpet's meaning

schools.summary: The unusual restriction: 'and it was commanded them that they should not hurt the grass of the earth, neither any green thing, neither any tree; but only those men which have not the seal of God in their foreheads' — unlike natural locusts (which devour vegetation), these forbidden to harm grass, green things, or trees, their target not the vegetable world but men (the restriction marking them as no ordinary locusts); the locusts acting under command (restrained and directed, their power bounded, able to hurt only what they are permitted, even the abyss-host under God's control); and able to hurt only those lacking the seal of God in their foreheads (the sealed servants of God, 7:3-4, protected, only the unsealed/ungodly tormented, the sealing of God's own shielding them from the plague) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they should not hurt the grass of the earth, neither any green thing, neither any tree
only those men which have not the seal of God in their foreheads
the locusts restrained to hurt only the unsealed
the sealed protected, the ungodly afflicted

schools.summary: The limited harm: 'and to them it was given (permitted) that they should not kill them, but that they should be tormented (basanisthosin — afflicted with pain) five months: and their torment was as the torment of a scorpion, when he striketh a man' — the locusts permitted to torment but not to kill (their power to inflict pain not death), and the torment limited to 'five months' (the natural lifespan/season of locusts, about five months, or symbolically a defined, bounded period, in the historicist reading a prophetic period); the pain like a scorpion's sting (acute and agonizing but not fatal, the figure of intense, tormenting affliction, severe but bounded) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they should not kill them, but that they should be tormented five months
their torment was as the torment of a scorpion, when he striketh a man
the locusts permitted to torment, not kill, for five months
severe but limited, non-fatal torment

schools.summary: The extremity of the torment: 'and in those days shall men seek death, and shall not find it; and shall desire to die, and death shall flee from them' — the torment so unbearable that men long for death as a release but death eludes them (the anguish driving them to seek to die, yet they cannot, the depth of the misery); the inability to find death heightening the horror (the sufferers unable to escape even by dying, the plague holding them in living torment from which death would be a mercy but is denied); 'death shall flee from them' — death, normally feared, now desired yet fleeing, the reversal marking the extremity of the woe, the torment worse than death yet death will not come (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: in those days shall men seek death, and shall not find it
shall desire to die, and death shall flee from them
men will seek death but not find it
a living anguish worse than death

schools.summary: The martial description (drawing on Joel 2:4): 'and the shapes of the locusts were like unto horses prepared unto battle; and on their heads were as it were crowns like gold, and their faces were as the faces of men' — the locusts appearing like war-horses arrayed for battle (the figure of a disciplined, advancing army, in the historicist reading the Arab cavalry); 'crowns like gold' (something resembling golden crowns) the symbol of victory, royalty, or conquest (the conquering, crowned host, the turbans of the Arabs to some historicists); 'faces as the faces of men' marking intelligence and (in the symbolic reading) that they represent men, a reasoning host not mere insects — the war-like, crowned, human-faced figure building the picture of a formidable army (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the shapes of the locusts were like unto horses prepared unto battle
crowns like gold, and their faces were as the faces of men
the war-like appearance of the locusts
the picture of a conquering, intelligent army

schools.summary: The further features: 'and they had hair as the hair of women, and their teeth were as the teeth of lions' — the locusts having long, flowing hair like women's and teeth like lions', the figures combining to portray a fierce, formidable host; 'hair as the hair of women' denoting long, flowing hair (some historicists seeing the long hair or beards of the Arabs, the figure of a wild, untamed, terrible appearance); 'teeth as the teeth of lions' (drawn from Joel 1:6) signifying devouring strength and ferocity (the locusts equipped to devour like lions, the figure of a fierce, ravenous power) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they had hair as the hair of women, and their teeth were as the teeth of lions
the locusts' womanly hair and lions' teeth
a wild, fierce, ravenous army
devouring strength and a terrible aspect

schools.summary: The further martial details: 'and they had breastplates, as it were breastplates of iron; and the sound of their wings was as the sound of chariots of many horses running to battle' — the locusts wearing what seem iron breastplates (the symbol of invulnerability, hardness, and military array, the host armed for war and hard to wound, the iron mail of the invading cavalry to some historicists), and their wings roaring like the rush of many chariots into battle (Joel 2:5, the overwhelming, terrifying noise of an advancing army, the din of war, the sound itself striking terror) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they had breastplates, as it were breastplates of iron
the sound of their wings was as the sound of chariots of many horses running to battle
the locusts' iron breastplates and the thunder of their wings
an invulnerable, overwhelming army

schools.summary: The stinging power: 'and they had tails like unto scorpions, and there were stings in their tails: and their power was to hurt men five months' — the locusts having scorpion-like tails with stings, their power to hurt residing in these tails, the affliction lasting the appointed five months (v.5); the 'stings in their tails' the instrument of the torment (in the historicist reading the stinging tail signifying the corrupting, tormenting power of the false religion left behind in its wake, the doctrine that stings and poisons, the lasting hurt in the tail); 'their power was to hurt men five months' again bounding the tormenting power to a defined, limited duration, the repetition stressing the bound (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: they had tails like unto scorpions, and there were stings in their tails
their power was to hurt men five months
the scorpion tails by which the locusts hurt men
the tormenting power residing in the tail, bounded in time

schools.summary: The ruler and his name: 'and they had a king over them, which is the angel of the bottomless pit (the abyss), whose name in the Hebrew tongue is Abaddon, but in the Greek tongue hath his name Apollyon' — unlike natural locusts (which 'have no king,' Proverbs 30:27), these ruled by a king, the angel/ruler of the abyss (the host having a directing head from the pit); the 'angel of the bottomless pit' the destroying power that governs the host (taken by many as Satan or a satanic angel, the presiding spirit of destruction; in the historicist reading, the leader or false prophet at the head of the invading false religion); the king's name 'Abaddon' (Hebrew) and 'Apollyon' (Greek) both meaning 'Destroyer'/'Destruction,' marking his character as the embodiment of ruin, the host he leads a destroying power (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: they had a king over them, which is the angel of the bottomless pit
whose name in the Hebrew tongue is Abaddon, but in the Greek tongue hath his name Apollyon
the king of the locusts, the angel of the abyss, named Destroyer
the destroying power at the head of the abyss-host

schools.summary: The structuring announcement: 'one woe is past; and, behold, there come two woes more hereafter' — the fifth trumpet (the locust-plague) being the first of three 'woe' trumpets (announced in 8:13), the first woe past and two more woes (the sixth and seventh trumpets) yet to come; 'one woe is past' signalling the end of the fifth-trumpet vision (the locust-torment run its course) and preparing for the sixth; 'two woes more hereafter' the sixth trumpet (the Euphratean horsemen, the second woe) and the seventh trumpet (the third woe) remaining, the worst not yet over, the announcement heightening expectation of the heavier judgments to come (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: One woe is past; and, behold, there come two woes more hereafter
the passing of the first woe and the coming of two more
the structure of the three woe-trumpets
the heavier judgments yet to come

schools.summary: The opening of the sixth trumpet: 'and the sixth angel sounded, and I heard a voice from the four horns of the golden altar which is before God' — at the sixth trumpet a voice coming from the golden altar of incense before God, the command for the next judgment issuing from the altar; the 'golden altar... before God' being the altar of incense (cf. 8:3), associated with the prayers of the saints; the voice from its horns suggesting that the coming judgment is, in some sense, an answer to the prayers of God's people for justice (cf. 6:10, the souls under the altar crying 'how long?') — the avenging act bound up with the prayers of the persecuted church (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: the sixth angel sounded, and I heard a voice from the four horns of the golden altar which is before God
the voice from the golden altar at the sixth trumpet
the altar of incense associated with the saints' prayers
the judgment answering the cries of the persecuted church

schools.summary: The command: 'saying to the sixth angel which had the trumpet, Loose (set free) the four angels which are bound in the great river Euphrates' — the altar-voice commanding the trumpet-angel to release four angels who had been bound (the loosing of restrained powers for judgment); the 'four angels bound' agents of judgment held back until the appointed time (whether evil angels, instruments of destruction, or the directing powers behind the invading host, now loosed); 'the great river Euphrates' the great eastern boundary, associated with the powers of the East (Assyria, Babylon, Parthia); the historicists reading the loosing of the Euphratean angels as the unleashing of the Turkish/Ottoman armies from the East upon Christendom, the powers behind the invasion long held in check (Barnes, Ellicott, Gill).

Victorinus: the four angels bound at the Euphrates are the four nations across the river (the four corners/winds of the earth), to each of which an angel is sent; they do not overpass their bounds, being held in check until at the last they come with Antichrist — the loosing marking the appointed unleashing of these nations in the final assault.

themes: Loose the four angels which are bound in the great river Euphrates
the command to loose the four angels bound at the Euphrates
the eastern powers loosed for judgment
the Ottoman armies (historicist) or the four nations beyond the river (Victorinus)

schools.summary: The precise appointment and lethal commission: 'and the four angels were loosed, which were prepared for (held ready against) an hour, and a day, and a month, and a year, for to slay the third part of men' — the angels prepared for a precisely appointed time, the accumulation of time-units (hour, day, month, year) stressing that the moment of judgment was exactly fixed by God (nothing left to chance); the phrase read either as simply that the angels were held ready for the exact appointed moment, or (in the historicist reading) as a prophetic period computed by some as the duration of the Ottoman power; and the angels loosed 'to slay the third part of men' — unlike the locusts (who only tormented), this second woe deadly (a third part of men slain, the judgment more severe, bringing death, the 'third part' marking a limited but vast destruction) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the four angels were loosed, which were prepared for an hour, and a day, and a month, and a year
for to slay the third part of men
the four angels loosed at the appointed time to slay a third of men
the exactly-timed, deadly judgment

schools.summary: The immense host: 'and the number of the army of the horsemen were two hundred thousand thousand (dismyriades myriadon — two myriads of myriads, two hundred million): and I heard the number of them' — the cavalry host numbered at two hundred million, an immense, almost innumerable army, John hearing the number declared; the number so vast as to signify an overwhelming, countless host (whether taken as a definite symbolic number or as a figure for an uncountable multitude, the historicists seeing the vast armies of the East, the staggering size marking the terror of the judgment); 'I heard the number of them' marking its definiteness in the vision (the immensity not vague but specified, beyond ordinary reckoning yet precisely numbered to God) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the number of the army of the horsemen were two hundred thousand thousand
I heard the number of them
the vast number of the horsemen — two hundred million
an overwhelming, countless host

schools.summary: The description: 'and thus I saw the horses in the vision, and them that sat on them, having breastplates of fire (fiery red), and of jacinth (hyacinth, dusky blue), and brimstone (sulphurous yellow): and the heads of the horses were as the heads of lions; and out of their mouths issued fire and smoke and brimstone' — the riders wearing breastplates of three colours (red, blue, yellow) matching the fire, smoke, and brimstone that issue from the horses (the terrible, hellish array); the horses lion-headed (the symbol of ferocity, strength, and destruction, an overwhelming devouring power); and breathing fire, smoke, and brimstone from their mouths (the instruments of the slaughter, v.18); the historicists seeing in the fire and smoke the gunpowder and artillery of the Ottoman armies (cannon and firearms), the deadly, hellish weaponry of the host (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the horses... having breastplates of fire, and of jacinth, and brimstone
the heads of the horses were as the heads of lions; and out of their mouths issued fire and smoke and brimstone
the fire-breathing horses with lions' heads
the deadly hellish weaponry (artillery, in the historicist reading)

schools.summary: The lethal effect: 'by these three was the third part of men killed, by the fire, and by the smoke, and by the brimstone, which issued out of their mouths' — the three plagues (fire, smoke, brimstone) issuing from the horses' mouths slaying a third part of men (the deadly commission, v.15, carried out, the second woe bringing death not mere torment); 'these three' named as distinct plagues, the instruments by which the third part is killed (the historicists seeing the destructive power of the invading armies, artillery and the devastation of war, the threefold deadly issue); 'the third part of men killed' — a third of mankind slain, the judgment severe and extensive yet bounded to a third, the 'third part' marking a partial, not total, destruction (a warning judgment, not the final end) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: by these three was the third part of men killed, by the fire, and by the smoke, and by the brimstone
the third of men killed by the fire, smoke, and brimstone
the deadly execution of the second woe
a severe but bounded, partial destruction

schools.summary: The twofold weapon: 'for their power is in their mouth, and in their tails: for their tails were like unto serpents, and had heads, and with them they do hurt' — the horses harming both with their mouths (the fire, smoke, brimstone, vv.17-18, the killing breath, the front of the host slaying) and with their tails (which are like serpents, with heads, that wound); a twofold instrument of hurt, the host harming not only in its frontal attack but in its rear; the historicists seeing in the serpent-tails a lasting, poisonous power left in the wake of the invasion (false doctrine or continuing harm), the twofold power making the host wholly deadly, deadly in front and behind (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: their power is in their mouth, and in their tails: for their tails were like unto serpents
with them they do hurt
the horses' power in their mouths and serpent tails
the host deadly in front and rear

schools.summary: The hardened survivors: 'and the rest of the men which were not killed by these plagues yet repented not of the works of their hands, that they should not worship devils, and idols of gold, and silver, and brass, and stone, and of wood: which neither can see, nor hear, nor walk' — those who survived the deadly judgment still not repenting (the plagues, severe as they were, not bringing them to repentance, the hardening of the impenitent under judgment); their persistent sin being demon-worship and idolatry (they would not repent so as to stop worshiping demons and idols of gold, silver, brass, stone, and wood, clinging to the very sins the judgment was meant to punish, the idolatry and superstition the plagues failed to reform); the idols 'which neither can see, nor hear, nor walk' the standard prophetic mockery of idols as dead, powerless, senseless things (Psalm 115:4-7), the folly of worshiping what is lifeless marking their hardness (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: the rest of the men which were not killed by these plagues yet repented not of the works of their hands
that they should not worship devils, and idols of gold, and silver, and brass, and stone, and of wood
the impenitence of the survivors who would not forsake their idolatry
the hardening of the impenitent under judgment

schools.summary: The catalogue of unrepented sins: 'neither repented they of their murders, nor of their sorceries (pharmakon — witchcraft, magic arts, drug-related occultism), nor of their fornication, nor of their thefts' — beyond their idolatry (v.20), the survivors not repenting of their other gross sins (murder, sorcery, fornication, theft, the catalogue of moral corruption and the works of the flesh they persisted in despite the judgment); the repeated 'neither repented they' stressing the hardness of the human heart under judgment — that even deadly plagues do not, of themselves, produce repentance, judgment alone not softening the impenitent, the heart needing to be changed by grace not merely terrified by wrath (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: Neither repented they of their murders, nor of their sorceries, nor of their fornication, nor of their thefts
the survivors' impenitence in murder, sorcery, fornication, and theft
the hardness of the heart under judgment
judgment alone not producing repentance
